description: Fix browser-context bugs found in the Plugin SDK audit: unhandled DOMException from invalid CSS selectors with timer/observer leaks in dom.ts, missing AbortSignal support in waitUntil, and missing test coverage for log module global registration.

userStories.title: Handle invalid CSS selectors and observer callback errors in DOM utilities
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want DOM utility functions to handle invalid CSS selectors gracefully so that a typo in a selector does not crash my tool handler and leak browser resources.
acceptanceCriteria: waitForSelector with an invalid CSS selector (e.g., '[invalid') rejects with a descriptive Error instead of throwing an unhandled DOMException, and does not leak a MutationObserver or timer
waitForSelectorRemoval with an invalid CSS selector rejects with a descriptive Error instead of throwing an unhandled DOMException, and does not leak a MutationObserver or timer
querySelectorAll with an invalid CSS selector returns an empty array instead of throwing an unhandled DOMException
getTextContent with an invalid CSS selector returns null instead of throwing an unhandled DOMException
observeDOM with an invalid CSS selector throws a descriptive Error (existing behavior preserved, but the error message should mention the selector)
In waitForSelector, if document.querySelector throws inside the MutationObserver callback, the timer is cleared and the observer is disconnected (no resource leak)
In waitForSelectorRemoval, if document.querySelector throws inside the MutationObserver callback, the timer is cleared and the observer is disconnected (no resource leak)
All new behaviors have corresponding unit tests in dom.test.ts
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files: platform/plugin-sdk/src/dom.ts, platform/plugin-sdk/src/dom.test.ts

Bug 1 — Invalid CSS selectors cause unhandled DOMException:
- document.querySelector() and document.querySelectorAll() throw DOMException with name 'SyntaxError' when given an invalid CSS selector
- All 5 exported functions call querySelector/querySelectorAll without try-catch
- For waitForSelector (line 37) and waitForSelectorRemoval (line 78): wrap the initial querySelector in try-catch, reject the promise with a descriptive error
- For the MutationObserver callbacks (lines 52 and 91): wrap the querySelector call in try-catch. On catch, do NOT reject — just return (ignore the mutation) since the initial check already handles invalid selectors. Actually wait — if the initial check succeeded (element not found), the observer is set up, and then the callback's querySelector won't throw for the same selector. The only way the callback's querySelector throws is if the selector was valid initially but somehow becomes invalid, which can't happen. So the callback-throws scenario is only reachable if querySelector semantics change mid-page, which is unrealistic. The real fix is just catching in the initial check.
- HOWEVER, there's a separate concern: what if querySelector inside the observer throws for reasons OTHER than an invalid selector? For example, if the document is in an unusual state. Wrapping the observer callback in try-catch is defensive and prevents the timer leak described in Bug 2.
- For querySelectorAll (line 112): wrap in try-catch, return empty array
- For getTextContent (line 119): wrap in try-catch, return null
- For observeDOM (line 135): the existing throw behavior is correct (caller needs to know), but wrap querySelector in try-catch and re-throw a descriptive Error

Bug 2 — Timer/observer leak in observer callbacks:
- In waitForSelector (lines 51-60): if the querySelector call on line 52 throws, lines 55-58 (clearTimeout, disconnect, resolve) are never reached. The settled flag is never set, so the timer continues running and eventually fires the timeout handler which does call disconnect. So the leak is temporary (until timeout fires) — the observer runs until timeout, wasting resources.
- Fix: wrap the observer callback body in try-catch. On error, just return (don't settle — let the timeout handle it, or optionally reject immediately).
- Same pattern in waitForSelectorRemoval (lines 91-98).

Test additions:
- Test waitForSelector with invalid selector rejects with descriptive error
- Test waitForSelectorRemoval with invalid selector rejects with descriptive error
- Test querySelectorAll with invalid selector returns []
- Test getTextContent with invalid selector returns null
- Test observeDOM with invalid selector throws (already tested, but verify error message mentions selector)
- Test that observer callback errors don't prevent cleanup

userStories.title: Add AbortSignal support to waitUntil
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want waitUntil to accept an AbortSignal so that I can cancel long-running polling operations when the MCP client cancels a request, matching the AbortSignal support already provided by retry.
acceptanceCriteria: WaitUntilOptions interface has an optional signal?: AbortSignal property
waitUntil rejects immediately if the signal is already aborted when called
waitUntil rejects promptly (within one polling interval) when the signal is aborted during polling
The polling timer is cleared and no further predicate calls are made after abort
The rejection error is the signal's reason (matching retry's behavior)
Existing behavior is preserved when no signal is provided
All new behaviors have corresponding unit tests in timing.test.ts
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files: platform/plugin-sdk/src/timing.ts, platform/plugin-sdk/src/timing.test.ts

The retry function (same file, line 35) already supports AbortSignal — follow its patterns:
- Add `signal?: AbortSignal` to WaitUntilOptions (line 18)
- At the start of waitUntil (line 98), check `if (signal?.aborted)` and reject immediately
- Register an abort listener that calls cleanup() and reject() with the abort reason
- In the cleanup function (line 104), also remove the abort listener
- Use the same abortReason() helper pattern as retry (line 47): if signal.reason is an Error use it, otherwise create new Error('waitUntil: aborted')

Follow retry's abortableSleep pattern for the abort listener setup — register with { once: true } and clean up on normal resolution.

Test additions (follow the retry abort test patterns at lines 147-233):
- Test waitUntil rejects immediately with already-aborted signal
- Test waitUntil rejects when signal is aborted during polling
- Test that abort stops further predicate calls
- Test that abort with a DOMException (default abort() behavior) produces the right error
- Test that abort with a custom Error reason preserves that reason

userStories.title: Add test for log module global registration on __openTabs
userStories.description: As a platform maintainer, I want a test verifying that the log module registers _setLogTransport and log on globalThis.__openTabs so that regressions in the adapter runtime integration are caught by unit tests.
acceptanceCriteria: A test verifies that globalThis.__openTabs._setLogTransport is a function after the log module is loaded
A test verifies that globalThis.__openTabs.log is the frozen log object (same reference as the exported log) after the log module is loaded
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
